meta.title: S211 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes E-Class S211 engine — M271, M112, M272, M113, M156, OM646, OM647, OM648, OM642, OM628, OM629. Real UK costs, honest reliability data. Compare prices from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes E-Class S211 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned S211 engine typically costs £2,200–£14,000+ supply only, depending on the engine — the volume OM646 diesel sits at £2,500–£4,500, while the M156 AMG unit ranges from £8,000–£14,000+. Fitting adds £650–£1,500. Use our calculator for a personalised figure.

mainEntity.name: Which Mercedes S211 engine is the most reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642.920 V6 diesel (E 320 CDI, 2005–2009) is widely regarded as the best engine of the entire S211 generation — it delivers 224 PS with 540 Nm of torque and regularly exceeds 400,000 kilometres with proper maintenance. The M112 V6 petrol (E 240/E 320) is also extremely robust and proven. The OM646 diesel is a reliable volume option.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes E-Class S211?

acceptedAnswer.text: M272/M273 balance shaft failure on pre-September 2006 engines, OM646 injector seal failure ('Black Death'), M271 timing chain stretch and supercharger clutch failure, SBC (Sensotronic Brake Control) pump failure on early cars (2002–2006), and — unrelated to the engine — self-levelling rear air suspension failure on the estate.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes E-Class S211 reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the engine and the year. The OM642 V6 diesel, M112 V6 petrol and M113 V8 are genuinely robust and well-documented, with many examples exceeding 200,000 miles. The OM646 diesel is also reliable but needs injector seal maintenance. The M272 and M273 have balance shaft issues on pre-2006 engines. The M271 Kompressor has known timing chain and supercharger issues.

mainEntity.name: Which S211 engine should I avoid?

acceptedAnswer.text: No engine outright — but pre-September 2006 M272/M273 engines (E 280, E 350, E 500) need careful checking for balance shaft history. The M271 Kompressor (E 200) has timing chain and supercharger issues that are expensive to fix. If you buy one, ensure the chain and tensioner have been replaced. The OM647 five-cylinder diesel is less refined than the OM646 and has injector and fuel pump concerns.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my S211 engine needs replacing?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain rattle or loss of boost (M271), check engine light with timing codes (M272/M273 balance shaft), rough idle or diesel smell (OM646/OM647 injector failure), or oil leaks from the valve covers (M113 V8) are key warnings. Use our diagnostic calculator for a personalised assessment.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes E-Class S211 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on the car's value. Against a well-kept £8,000+ S211 (especially a V8 or AMG), a £3,000–£4,500 recon is 38–56% of the car's value — safe. Against a tired £3,000 example with air suspension issues, a £3,000+ recon may exceed the car's worth — walk away. Our 70% Mercedes threshold is the line. For the OM642, a recon is usually a once-in-a-lifetime event as these engines regularly exceed 400,000 kilometres.

mainEntity.name: How long do Mercedes E-Class S211 engines last?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM642 V6 diesel, M112 V6 and M113 V8 can exceed 400,000 kilometres (250,000 miles) with proper servicing. The OM646 diesel and OM648 straight-six are typically good for 200,000+ miles. The M272/M273 are good for 150,000+ miles if the balance shaft has been addressed. The M271 is the shortest-lived due to its timing chain and supercharger issues. The M156 AMG is typically good for 80,000–120,000 miles if maintained to AMG standards.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between the S211 and W211?

acceptedAnswer.text: Both are the third-generation E-Class. The S211 is the 5-door estate (station wagon, 2002–2009), while the W211 is the 4-door saloon (2002–2009). They share the same platform, engines, and most components — but the estate has self-levelling rear air suspension as standard and additional load-carrying capacity. Costs vary slightly due to the air suspension system — see the W211 generation page for specifics.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes E-Class S211 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation; the engine code is also stamped on the block. Tell us your variant (e.g., E 320 CDI, E 220 CDI, E 500) and registration year, and we'll identify it — 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists will confirm it for free.

hero.h1: Mercedes E-Class S211 Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the S211 estate — from the M271 Kompressor four-cylinders to the M112 and M272 V6 petrols, the M113 and M156 V8s, and the OM646, OM647, OM648 and OM642 diesels. Real UK replacement costs, honest failure data, and the balance-shaft and injector issues that define this generation's ownership story. Backed by 338+ real UK E-Class enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

engineDatabase.h2: Mercedes E-Class S211 Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every engine fitted to the S211 estate specifically — from the entry-level four-cylinder Kompressor to the fire-breathing M156 V8 in the E 63 AMG.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM642 V6 diesel: 90% confidence — widely regarded as the best engine of the entire S211 generation, with mileages regularly exceeding 400,000 kilometres. M112 V6 and M113 V8: 85% confidence — among the most durable Mercedes engines ever built. OM646 and OM648: 80% confidence — proven diesel families with strong longevity. M272 V6: 75% confidence — early units (pre-September 2006) suffer from balance shaft issues. M273 V8: 75% confidence — similar balance shaft concerns to the M272. M271: 75% confidence — known for timing chain and supercharger issues. OM647: 75% confidence — five-cylinder diesel with injector and fuel pump concerns. OM628/OM629 V8 diesels: 70% confidence — rare and specialist, with higher running costs.

overview.intro: The S211 is the estate body style of the third-generation E-Class, launched in February 2002 as the successor to the S210 estate. Based on the W211 platform, it offered a wider range of engines than its predecessor, with petrol and diesel options spanning four-cylinder, V6, V8 and even V8 diesel configurations. A major 2006 facelift brought revised styling, the M272 and M273 V6/V8 petrols, and the OM642 V6 diesel that replaced the OM648 straight-six. The range-topping E 63 AMG, introduced in 2006, used the M156 6.2-litre V8 producing 514 PS. The S211 estate features self-levelling rear air suspension as standard, a system that requires careful maintenance on higher-mileage examples.
overview.keyFacts: Platform: Mercedes W211 • Predecessor: S210 • Successor: S212 • Sibling body style in this generation: W211 Saloon.
overview.marketIntelligenceLine: The S211 E-Class estate generated 338 real UK engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], concentrated on the OM642 diesel and M112/M272 V6 petrols as these cars enter enthusiast-owner territory.

items.quote: The 3.0-litre V6 diesel OM642 is the standout engine of the entire S211 generation. It delivers 224 PS with 540 Nm of torque, combines power with refinement and economy, and regularly exceeds 400,000 kilometres with proper maintenance. Early OM642 units had some timing chain and injector issues, but post-2005 variants with optimised common-rail injection proved exceptionally durable.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want the best combination of performance, economy and reliability in the S211 range.

items.quote: The 3.2-litre M112 V6 is arguably the most bulletproof petrol engine ever fitted to an E-Class. 221 PS of smooth, naturally aspirated power, no balance-shaft issues, no timing-chain worries — just a robust, proven V6 that keeps going. The 2.6-litre M112.917 in the E 240 is equally solid.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V6 smoothness without the M272's balance-shaft risk.

items.quote: The M272 V6 is a great engine — but early units (before September 2006) suffer from a well-documented balance shaft gear failure. The bearings fail between 100,000 and 150,000 miles, causing the timing to jump and potentially destroying the engine. Post-2006 engines have the revised parts and are much more reliable. If you're buying a pre-2006 E 280 or E 350, check the engine number against Mercedes' affected range.
items.whoItsFor: buyers willing to check engine numbers and budget for balance shaft replacement, or those buying post-2006 examples.

items.quote: The 6.2-litre M156 V8 is a masterpiece — 514 PS of naturally aspirated AMG thunder. But when it fails, it fails expensively. Camshaft adjusters, lifters, and the sheer cost of AMG-specific parts mean replacement bills frequently hit five figures. The E 55 AMG's supercharged M113 V8 is cheaper to maintain.
items.whoItsFor: collectors and enthusiasts with deep pockets.

items.quote: The 2.1-litre OM646 offers 170 PS with genuine 50+ mpg economy and a proven 200,000-mile-plus lifespan when properly maintained. It's cheaper to buy, cheaper to insure, and cheaper to replace than the V6 diesels — the sweet spot in the S211 diesel range for most UK buyers.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want diesel economy without the V6's complexity or running costs.

items.quote: The 5.0-litre M113 V8 is often overlooked in favour of the AMG models, but it offers 306 PS of smooth, reliable V8 power without the supercharger complexity or the M156's eye-watering running costs. It's the S211's sleeper pick — a V8 that sounds wonderful and keeps going.
items.whoItsFor: buyers who want V8 refinement without the AMG tax.

rows.ownershipVerdict: Best Petrol — bulletproof V6, no major issues.
Facelift V6 — great engine, but check balance shaft.
AMG V8 — reliable, but expensive when it fails.
Most Expensive — AMG masterpiece, AMG bills.
Straight-Six Diesel — smooth, reliable, replaced by OM642.
Best Diesel — the S211's most durable engine.
V8 Diesel — rare, powerful, specialist territory.

economicsRule.title: The S211 rule
economicsRule.text: A £2,500–£4,500 OM646 recon against a £4,000 S211 sits at 63–113% of the car's value. On a sub-£4,000 S211, that brushes our 70% Mercedes threshold — we'll honestly tell you to walk away. Against a well-kept £8,000+ example (particularly a V8 or AMG), the same recon drops to a routine 31–56% — a repair decision, not a financial one. Value the car first. For the OM642, the maths is similar — but these engines regularly exceed 400,000 kilometres, so a recon is usually a once-in-a-lifetime event.

problems.title: M272/M273 Balance Shaft Failure
problems.affectedModels: E 230, E 280, E 350, E 500 (M272/M273 V6/V8 engines, pre-September 2006).
problems.rootCause: The M272 and M273 engines have a well-documented balance shaft gear failure that causes the timing to jump, leading to engine damage. This affects engines built before August 2006. Post-2006 engines have the revised parts and are much more reliable.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying a pre-2006 S211 with an M272 or M273 engine, check the engine number against Mercedes' affected range. If it's affected, budget for balance shaft replacement or walk away. A £1,000–£1,800 preventative repair is far cheaper than the £3,500–£6,500 replacement that follows a complete failure. A ticking noise that gets louder over weeks suggests cam-adjuster wear progressing toward failure.

cta.label: Compare M272/M273 engine prices for your S211 E 280/350/500 →

problems.title: M271 Timing Chain & Supercharger Clutch Failure
problems.rootCause: The M271's timing chain tensioner can fail, leading to chain stretch and potentially catastrophic valve damage. The supercharger clutch is also a known weak point — failure causes loss of boost and a rattling noise. Camshaft adjuster wear is another common issue.
problems.recommendation: On any M271-powered S211 over 60,000 miles, have the timing chain tensioner and supercharger clutch inspected at every service — £600–£1,200 now is far cheaper than the £2,200–£4,000+ replacement that follows a skipped chain.

cta.label: Compare M271 engine prices for your S211 E 200 KOMPRESSOR →

problems.title: OM646 Injector Seal Failure (Black Death)
problems.rootCause: The OM646's injector seals can fail, allowing combustion gases to leak past — this creates carbon buildup (the 'Black Death' effect) and eventually causes injector seizure and cylinder damage. Intermittent misfire between idle and 1,500 rpm is a common symptom.
problems.recommendation: At the first sign of rough idle, diesel smell in the cabin, or intermittent misfire, get the injector seals inspected immediately — a £400–£800 repair is dramatically cheaper than the £2,500–£4,500 replacement that follows if ignored.

cta.label: Compare OM646 engine prices for your S211 E 200/220 CDI →

problems.title: S211 Self-Levelling Rear Air Suspension Failure
problems.rootCause: The S211 estate uses self-levelling rear air suspension as standard. The rubber air springs perish over time and crack, allowing air to leak. The compressor, ride height sensors, and pipework can also fail. Moisture ingress and corrosion in the compressor connector is a known cause.
problems.recommendation: Have the rear air suspension inspected on any S211 — a £200–£500 inspection is far cheaper than the £1,500–£3,000+ replacement that follows a complete system failure. Check for the car sitting low overnight or unevenly, as these are early warning signs.

cta.label: Compare S211 rear air suspension repair quotes →

problems.title: SBC (Sensotronic Brake Control) Failure (Early Cars)
problems.rootCause: Early W211/S211 cars use SBC (Sensotronic Brake Control), an electro-hydraulic braking system. The SBC pump has a limited number of brake pedal presses before it shuts itself off. This is a known issue on early cars.
problems.recommendation: If you're buying an early S211 (2002–2006), check the SBC pump history. Mercedes extended the warranty on SBC pumps for many markets, but this may have expired. A failed SBC pump is a dealer-only repair.

replacementCosts.h2: S211 Engine Replacement Costs — By Engine and Condition
replacementCosts.note: No Generation variance from the Model Page's baseline figures — S211 estate shares its W211-derived engine bay with the saloon, so costs are inherited directly per the Authority Model, with no divergence note required. M156 AMG costs reflect AMG-specific parts and specialist labour requirements.

coreVariants.scopeNote: All variants listed are S211 estate only. W211 Saloon shares most of these engines but is covered on its own Generation Page, as fitment and labour access vary slightly by body style. The S211's self-levelling rear air suspension is unique to the estate and not present on the saloon.

mostRequestedEngines: 1. OM642.920 (E 320 CDI) — V6 diesel favourite [PATTERN DATA]
3. M112.949 (E 320) — V6 petrol enthusiast choice [PATTERN DATA]
